title: Terminal Inside Vim
subtitle: `:terminal` opens a real shell — usable with Vim navigation.
summary: `:terminal` (or `:term`) opens a buffer running an interactive shell. You can switch to *normal mode* inside the terminal to scroll back, search, and yank with normal Vim motions.

blocks.text: `:terminal` (or `:term`) opens a real interactive shell *inside a Vim buffer*. It's a buffer like any other — split it, switch to it, yank from it.

blocks.title: Two States: Terminal-Job and Terminal-Normal
blocks.text:
A terminal buffer has two modes:

  • *Terminal-Job* — keystrokes go to the shell process. This is the default — feels like a normal terminal.
  • *Terminal-Normal* — keystrokes are interpreted by Vim. You can scroll back through output, search with {key:/}, yank text with {key:y}, etc.

Switch to Terminal-Normal with {key:Ctrl-W}{key:N} (or {key:Ctrl-\}{key:Ctrl-N} in Vim 8, or just {key:Esc} in Neovim). Switch back with {key:i} or {key:a} (just like *normal mode* → *insert mode*).

This means your terminal buffer's scrollback is searchable and yankable using all your Vim muscle memory. Saw an error message? {key:Ctrl-W}{key:N} {key:?}`error`{key:Enter} {key:y}{key:y} to yank the line.

Note: {key:Ctrl-W} in Terminal-Job is the *window prefix*, not delete-word. To send literal {key:Ctrl-W} to the shell, prefix with {key:Ctrl-W}{key:.}.
